data.title: Pulse Biosciences targets CE Mark submission in 2026 as nPulse platform advances clinical milestones
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Pulse Biosciences, Inc. (PLSE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Paul LaViolette stated that Pulse Biosciences is “creating a pulsed field ablation platform to completely shift how physicians treat disease” by advancing nanosecond pulsed field ablation (nsPFA), which he described as “potential to completely disrupt multiple soft tissue ablation markets.” LaViolette highlighted progress in 2025, including expanded patient treatment in European feasibility studies, IDE approvals for both electrophysiology and cardiac surgical clamp programs, and a controlled launch of the Vybrance platform for soft tissue ablation. He also emphasized the company’s intellectual property position, noting the addition of 67 issued and 77 pending patents in 2025, totaling 250 granted and 180 pending patents.
  • LaViolette reported that key clinical objectives remain the advancement of the nsPFA platform into late-stage clinical development for atrial fibrillation treatment and the exploration of the soft tissue ablation system’s launch feasibility ahead of specific therapeutic claims. He stated, “Our nPulse cardiac catheter can treat a targeted area of the heart with a 5-second single-shot burst, delivering circumferential pulmonary vein isolation or PVI.”
  • LaViolette described the Vybrance platform’s commercial rollout as “highly controlled” and focused on a few select institutions, noting, “We fully appreciate the essential value of FDA indication clearance as well as reimbursement certainty. We anticipate this to be a worthwhile work in progress over the next 4 to 8 quarters.”
  • CFO Jon Skinner stated, “In the fourth quarter, we generated nominal revenues comprised of both nPulse Capital and Vybrance disposable sales. Total revenue was $264,000, up from $86,000 in Q3. This sequential growth was driven by both capital and disposable devices.” Skinner also noted, “Total GAAP costs and expenses decreased by $1.7 million to $18.5 million compared to $20.3 million in the prior year period,” primarily due to a reduction in nonrecurring expenses.

Outlook

  • LaViolette confirmed the company intends to commence and complete enrollment in the nPulse cardiac catheter IDE study for atrial fibrillation in 2026, continue European treatment to support CE Mark submission, and expand IDE site activation for the cardiac clamp system. He indicated, “We expect to use the data from our European feasibility study to finalize our CE submission in the second half of 2026 with the potential for CE Mark approval in 2027.”
  • For Vybrance, LaViolette said the PRECISE benign thyroid nodule study is on track to complete enrollment of 50 patients in the next few months with a plan to expand to 100 patients over the following two quarters.
  • The company anticipates further expansion of its collaboration with MD Anderson Cancer Center for IDE studies on benign and malignant thyroid tumors, targeting enrollment completion by year-end 2026.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for Q4 was $264,000, up from $86,000 in Q3, driven by both capital and disposable device sales. Cost of product revenue was $260,000 for the quarter, slightly lower on a sequential basis.
  • Total GAAP costs and expenses were $18.5 million compared to $20.3 million in the prior year period. Non-GAAP costs and expenses increased to $13.3 million from $11.3 million in the prior year period due to increased clinical trial and commercial activity.
  • GAAP net loss for Q4 was ($17.4 million) compared to ($19.4 million) in the prior year period. Non-GAAP net loss was ($12.2 million) compared to ($10.4 million) in the prior year period.
  • Cash and cash equivalents stood at $80.7 million as of December 31, 2025, compared to $118 million at the end of 2024. Cash used in operating activities was $14.8 million in Q4 compared to $13 million in Q3.
  • Skinner highlighted a $200 million shelf registration, stating, “This provides the company with flexibility to support the balance sheet in an expeditious manner to ensure we have the resources required to achieve upcoming clinical milestones.”

Q&A

  • Anthony Petrone, Mizuho Securities: Asked about the timeline for transitioning Vybrance from limited to broader release and for enrollment in the thyroid study. LaViolette replied, “Vybrance is exactly where we want it to be right now. It is in a market development mode... We are evaluating exactly how it works and exactly what we need to really scale it.” He confirmed enrollment completion for the PRECISE study is on track in the next 1 to 2 months, with plans to expand to 100 patients.
  • Petrone asked about nPulse reception and IDE study acceleration. LaViolette stated that “receptivity to the data... has been exceptionally positive,” and expects to commence and complete enrollment in 2026, prioritizing program acceleration.
  • Suraj Kalia, Oppenheimer: Asked about mapping system integration for nPulse, next-gen nPulse, and NANOCLAMP synergies. LaViolette said, “We do expect to have improved software integration in the IDE, number one,” and next-gen nPulse is still in development. He noted, “We know that there are real synergies as we generate great data in each application and both of those data sets go into the FDA to essentially comparable review teams on nearly overlapping time lines.”
  • Joshua Jennings, TD Cowen: Inquired about mechanisms behind clinical results and timing for feasibility updates. LaViolette emphasized the importance of replicating clinical results, with the next data event planned for HRS.
  • Jesse Crawford, Luxury Lifestyle Design: Asked about patient trial participation and partnership strategy. Robert Duggan explained, “We do not have a label from anything other than soft tissue ablation... we do not have reimbursement. Paul said... that will come over the next 4 to 8 quarters.” Duggan also mentioned funding could come from partnerships or distribution agreements.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed optimism about recent clinical data but focused questions on commercialization timing, broader market access, mapping integration, and partnership strategy. Tone was positive with some pressing on timelines and expansion.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone, emphasizing deliberate scaling, foundational market development, and the importance of data. LaViolette used phrases such as “We feel very good about completing that enrollment on time” and “We are really focused on ensuring that we build that foundation because growth in patient treatments... will happen once we build the fundamentals.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management sentiment was more confident, driven by new clinical data and increased revenue, but management continued to stress the need for careful, data-driven growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue increased to $264,000 in Q4 from $86,000 in Q3, reflecting early commercial traction. Cash use in operating activities also rose.
  • Clinical progress advanced, with IDE approvals and expanding patient enrollment across all platforms compared to Q3’s focus on initial launches and feasibility studies.
  • Management’s tone evolved from cautious optimism in Q3 to stronger confidence about regulatory and commercial milestones in Q4.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from initial development and study criteria in Q3 to commercialization scale, partnership prospects, and integration strategies in Q4.
  • Strategic priorities remained focused on clinical validation, market foundation, and regulatory progress, but the company signaled readiness for broader scale pending further data and FDA clearance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified the lack of FDA indication clearance and reimbursement certainty for Vybrance as ongoing challenges, with expectations for progress in the next 4 to 8 quarters.
  • Duggan acknowledged a “touch of frustration on the Vybrance side where the singular importance of being able to achieve a label has been long coming,” highlighting dependence on regulatory and reimbursement milestones.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the pace of commercialization, integration with mapping technologies, and funding for advancing clinical programs. Management pointed to a $200 million shelf registration and potential partnerships as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Pulse Biosciences emphasized its progress in advancing nanosecond PFA technology into late-stage clinical development, reporting positive clinical milestones, increased revenue, and a strengthened IP portfolio. Management reiterated a deliberate approach to commercialization, with foundational work underway in anticipation of regulatory approvals and reimbursement, and signaled that pivotal enrollment and CE Mark submissions are key value drivers for 2026. The company remains focused on executing clinical and market development priorities, supported by flexible funding options and ongoing partnership discussions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ardelyx outlines $1B IBSRELA revenue target by 2029 while advancing CIC label expansion and new pipeline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ardelyx, Inc. (ARDX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Michael Raab, President, CEO & Director, stated, "2025 was an extraordinary year for the company as the team delivered on every single one of our strategic priorities. That performance establishes a strong foundation for what we will accomplish in 2026." Raab highlighted the acceleration of IBSRELA growth momentum, execution on the XPHOZAH strategy, pipeline expansion, and strong financial performance. He emphasized, "IBSRELA is now helping tens of thousands of patients, evidenced by the incredible revenue growth of 73% compared to 2024 and 61% year-over-year growth in the fourth quarter."
  • Raab announced a new patent for the commercial formulations of IBSRELA and XPHOZAH, expiring in 2042 and now listed in the Orange Book at the FDA, as "an important component of our strategy to create additional valuable intellectual property."
  • The company launched two development programs, including the Phase III program to expand the IBSRELA label to include chronic idiopathic constipation (CIC) and the next-generation NHE3 inhibitor, RDX-10531 (531), aiming to further strengthen the portfolio.
  • Raab stated, "With the guidance we shared in January, IBSRELA is clearly demonstrating its blockbuster potential and is on track to deliver $1 billion in revenue in 2029 with significant growth thereafter."
  • Sue Hohenleitner, Chief Financial Officer, said, "We had significant year-over-year total revenue growth of 22% with full year 2025 revenues of $407.3 million compared to $333.6 million in 2024. That growth was driven by a significant increase in IBSRELA demand, which grew revenues to $274.2 million, an increase of 73% compared to the full year of 2024."

Outlook

  • Hohenleitner provided guidance for 2026, stating, "We continue to anticipate 2026 revenues for IBSRELA to be between $410 million and $430 million. That represents at least 50% year-over-year growth at the low end of the guidance range."
  • XPHOZAH revenues for 2026 are expected to be between $110 million and $120 million.
  • The company reaffirmed longer-term expectations of $1 billion in IBSRELA revenue in 2029 and $750 million for XPHOZAH before patent expiration.
  • Overall 2026 operating expenses are expected to increase by approximately 25% for total OpEx of up to $520 million, attributed to pipeline advancement and commercial growth investments.

Financial Results

  • Ardelyx reported full year 2025 revenues of $407.3 million, up from $333.6 million in 2024, driven by IBSRELA revenue of $274.2 million and XPHOZAH revenue of $103.6 million.
  • The company ended 2025 with $264.7 million in total cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments, compared to $250.1 million at the end of 2024.
  • Research and development expenses for 2025 were $71.5 million, while selling, general and administrative expenses were $337.2 million.
  • Net loss for the full year 2025 was $61.6 million or ($0.26) per share.

Q&A

  • Anthea Li, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: Asked about confidence in IBSRELA volume growth for guidance. Raab responded, "We have great confidence in reaching that number... our confidence is significant and hasn't wavered." Foster added, "We're continuing to optimize our sales force... we are going to double our field reimbursement manager team."
  • Allison Bratzel, Piper Sandler & Co.: Inquired about OpEx increases and commercial infrastructure. Hohenleitner said, "We are going to be increasing our OpEx about 25% year-over-year... these investments that we're making, this is really all about growth." Foster added, "We will be doubling the size of the field reimbursement team."
  • Samuel Alexander Leach, Raymond James: Asked about CIC trial dosing strategy. Williams explained, "We want to... actually look at a dose that we don't expect to provide as much... CIC seems to be the less difficult condition to treat."
  • Jin-Wook Kim, Citigroup Inc.: Sought color on additional patent protection. Raab stated, "Our job... is to continue to strengthen our intellectual property position for products like XPHOZAH/IBSRELA and that's what we're continuing to do."
  • Wing Yip, Wedbush Securities Inc.: Questioned the role of frontline use in IBSRELA projections. Raab replied, "Going after frontline is not an objective that we have and is not factored into the numbers."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest in the sustainability of high growth, operational discipline, and IP protection, with a generally positive tone but probing for risk factors and details on investment pacing.
  • Management projected significant confidence in growth, pipeline progress, and commercial execution, using phrases like "our confidence is significant," and "we feel really good about 2026."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, while analysts continued to probe for durability and execution risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for IBSRELA was raised, with a new revenue target of $1 billion by 2029 highlighted in this quarter.
  • Strategic focus broadened with the launch of the CIC label expansion program and the next-generation NHE3 inhibitor.
  • Management tone remained highly confident, emphasizing operational discipline and IP expansion, while addressing new growth initiatives more explicitly than in the previous quarter.
  • Analysts remained focused on growth sustainability, commercialization investments, and pipeline execution, with similar themes to the prior quarter but increased focus on long-term differentiation and patent strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the loss of Part D reimbursement for Medicare patients on XPHOZAH and emphasized their focus on non-Medicare revenue segments and patient access strategies.
  • Increased operating expenses in 2026 are planned, with management noting these are high-ROI investments to drive future growth.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of growth, need for infrastructure expansion, and the impact of payer hurdles, while management outlined mitigation strategies such as field sales optimization and expanded reimbursement teams.

Final Takeaway

Ardelyx management emphasized strong momentum in both IBSRELA and XPHOZAH, highlighted by a 73% annual revenue increase for IBSRELA and new long-term targets, including $1 billion in IBSRELA revenue by 2029. The company is advancing its pipeline with a CIC label expansion for IBSRELA and a next-generation NHE3 inhibitor, while navigating reimbursement changes and investing in commercial growth. Management expressed high confidence in delivering on these goals, supported by a strengthened intellectual property position and a robust financial footing heading into 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Harmonic outlines $440M–$480M 2026 broadband revenue target as pure-play transformation accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Harmonic Inc. (HLIT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Nimrod Ben-Natan, President and CEO, stated that "we delivered a strong fourth quarter, reflecting accelerating momentum across our broadband business." He announced the pending sale of the Video business to MediaKind, describing it as "a decisive step that will push our growth strategy forward and transform us into... a pure-play broadband leader." Ben-Natan emphasized that the transaction will simplify Harmonic's operating model and align resources to unlock growth in the expanding broadband infrastructure market.
  • Ben-Natan reported, "Broadband revenue for the quarter was $98.2 million, representing 9% sequential growth and coming in above the high end of guidance." He highlighted record quarterly bookings of $346.9 million and a backlog and deferred revenue of $573.8 million at year-end, stating this was "up 73% year-over-year."
  • Ben-Natan underscored the significance of customer diversification, noting that rest-of-world revenue grew 33% year-over-year and now represents 41% of total broadband revenue. He cited growing international wins and expanding platform relationships, with major new deployments in Europe and with Telia in Norway. He pointed to continued scaling of Harmonic's fiber business and highlighted a multiyear fiber expansion deal with izzi, the largest MSO in Mexico.
  • Ben-Natan described the convergence of fiber and DOCSIS within Harmonic’s cloud-native architecture as a key differentiator, allowing operators to manage both technologies through a unified platform. He mentioned successful DOCSIS 4.0 field validation with Vodafone Germany and stated that "Unified DOCSIS 4.0 node shipments are now ramping with initial deliveries this quarter."
  • Ben-Natan said, "We now have 146 COS deployments in production, serving more than 41 million cable modems and ONUs worldwide," and cited a Net Promoter Score of 82.
  • CFO Walter Jankovic stated, "For total company results, including Video discontinued operations, revenue was $157.3 million, EPS was $0.14 and adjusted EBITDA was $23.8 million, all well above our Q4 guidance." For continuing operations, he reported Q4 broadband revenue of $98.2 million, adjusted EBITDA of $12.1 million, and EPS of $0.06. Jankovic noted Rest-of-World revenue as representing 41% of total revenue and recurring revenue comprising 16% of broadband revenue.

Outlook

  • Jankovic guided for Q1 2026 broadband revenue of $100 million to $105 million, gross margins between 54% to 55%, operating profit between $18 million to $20 million, and EPS of $0.11 to $0.12. For the full year 2026, he projected broadband revenue between $440 million to $480 million, gross margins between 51% to 53%, operating profit between $74 million to $99 million, and EPS of $0.46 to $0.63. He noted that "operating profit includes stranded costs of approximately $10 million."
  • Jankovic explained, "With our 2026 guidance, we're taking a prudent and measured approach on both revenue and margins, considering factors such as the current memory chip pricing and supply dynamics."

Financial Results

  • Jankovic reported that Q4 broadband revenue was $98.2 million, above the $85 million to $95 million guidance range. Adjusted EBITDA reached $12.1 million and EPS was $0.06. He emphasized strong broadband bookings of $346.9 million, resulting in a 3.5 book-to-bill ratio. Backlog and deferred revenue stood at $573.8 million, with $307 million expected to convert to revenue within 12 months.
  • For the full year, total company net revenue was $570.8 million, gross margin was 55.8%, adjusted EBITDA was $83.8 million, and EPS was $0.47. Continuing operations generated $360.5 million in revenue, a 48.7% gross margin, adjusted EBITDA of $47.3 million, and EPS of $0.23. Free cash flow during Q4 was $9.6 million, and cash and cash equivalents at year-end were $124.1 million.
  • Jankovic stated that "recurring revenue reflected in the services and SaaS revenue line item made up 16% of our total continuing operations or broadband revenue."

Q&A

  • Simon Leopold, Raymond James: Asked about customer mix for 2026 and the sustainability of rest-of-world revenue at 40%. Jankovic responded that "Rest-of-World and the expected continued growth at 30% plus is one of the factors in looking at the absolute growth," but quarter-to-quarter variation is expected.
  • Leopold questioned the impact of memory costs on gross margin. Jankovic said, "We've built in around a net $6 million impact as a result of the pricing that we see today," and highlighted potential timing risks in delivery and customer ecosystem impacts.
  • Ryan Koontz, Needham & Company: Asked about the strength and composition of Q4 bookings. Jankovic indicated "it was very strong in rest of world" and included multiyear bookings, while Ben-Natan added that ramp-up in customer connection rates was driven by new deployments announced in 2025.
  • Taran Katta, Wolfe Research: Sought clarification on the $6 million net memory cost and pricing actions. Jankovic confirmed the figure and explained that "the pricing actions have to do with certain products that obviously have higher memory content."
  • Steven Frankel, Rosenblatt Securities: Asked about the mix of bookings and SaaS/service revenue. Jankovic stated, "It was the large majority in terms of SLA contracts," with Ben-Natan noting an expected increase in SaaS and intelligence-driven tools as recurring revenue drivers.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on the sustainability of rest-of-world growth, implications of memory cost headwinds, and the mix of hardware versus software in bookings, indicating a neutral to slightly positive outlook but with a focus on risk factors.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, emphasizing visibility from record bookings and backlog, but acknowledged headwinds such as memory costs and delivery timing. Ben-Natan stated, "These dynamics give us confidence in our long-term growth trajectory as DOCSIS 4.0 and fiber deployment scale through 2026 and beyond."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence was more pronounced, supported by the pending Video sale and significant backlog, while analysts maintained a cautious but constructive stance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q4 call introduced the strategic sale of the Video business and the transformation to a pure-play broadband company, a major shift from the prior quarter’s focus on both broadband and video segments.
  • Q4 showcased a significant increase in broadband backlog and bookings, customer diversification, and rest-of-world revenue mix, compared to Q3’s emphasis on initial DOCSIS 4.0 adoption and fiber deployments.
  • Management’s tone was more assertive regarding growth visibility for 2026, with explicit full-year revenue and margin targets. Analysts continued to focus on customer concentration, supply chain risks, and the scaling of software-driven recurring revenue.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted memory chip pricing and supply dynamics as a headwind, with Jankovic noting, "we've built in around a net $6 million impact as a result of the pricing that we see today."
  • Jankovic pointed to potential risks from supply chain delays and broader ecosystem impacts that could affect revenue timing.
  • Stranded costs following the Video business sale were identified, with an expectation of approximately $10 million for 2026.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of rest-of-world growth rates and the impact of product mix on gross margins.

Final Takeaway

Harmonic’s fourth quarter marked a pivotal transition as the company moves toward becoming a pure-play broadband leader, underpinned by record broadband bookings, substantial backlog, and expanding customer diversification. The pending sale of the Video business and robust guidance for 2026 position Harmonic to capitalize on the accelerating modernization of broadband networks globally, while ongoing investments in unified DOCSIS and fiber technologies, alongside intelligence-driven software, are expected to drive growth, recurring revenue, and margin expansion in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Indie Semiconductor outlines 20% sequential core revenue growth trajectory as radar and vision programs accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: indie Semiconductor (INDI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Donald McClymont stated that "indie delivered a solid fourth quarter with revenue of $58 million, exceeding the midpoint of our outlook by $1 million and up 8% sequentially." He highlighted the automotive sector's transition as ADAS and automated driving features become standard, creating "a significant opportunity for indie to capitalize on by leveraging its technology investments for the readiness of these mass market ADAS segments."
  • McClymont announced initial shipments to a Tier 1 partner for the Gen 8 77-gigahertz radar solution, noting "strong commercial traction with even more global OEMs," and emphasized scaling production to address demand that could exceed 50 million units annually post ramp-up. He also described expanding production capabilities and securing additional back-end and test capacity to meet demand.
  • The CEO reported momentum in the vision portfolio, citing a design win in China with an EV manufacturer for the iND880 camera mirror system, expected to ramp in mid-2026. He highlighted the integration of emotion3D, creating "a powerful ecosystem that unites AI-based perception algorithms with our hardware SoC capabilities."
  • New markets are being pursued, with McClymont noting, "we are already seeing strong adoption of our radar, vision and even interface solutions by industry leaders" in humanoid robotics and quantum communications. The photonics unit secured its largest booking of LXM lasers to date, and the power group remains on track for production with Ford in 2026 for the Qi 2.0 wireless platform.
  • McClymont referenced supply constraints: "we have made meaningful progress by qualifying second source package and substrate vendors. However, we expect the broader supply environment to remain constrained, and we will need to remain laser focused to manage the situation through 2026."
  • CFO Naixi Wu reported, "indie's fourth quarter revenue was $58 million, exceeding the midpoint of our outlook by $1 million, representing sequential growth of approximately 8% and flat compared to the prior year period, bringing our full year revenue to $217.4 million."
  • Wu stated, "The non-GAAP operating expenses during the quarter totaled $36.8 million, consistent with our outlook, thereby achieving our goal of $8 million to $10 million savings." She reported a non-GAAP operating loss of $10.1 million, net loss of $12.4 million, and loss per share of $0.07. Cash and equivalents at quarter end were $155.7 million.
  • Wu discussed the sale of Wuxi indie Micro for "gross proceeds of approximately $135 million, payable in cash upon closing," which is expected to close by late 2026, subject to regulatory approval in China.

Outlook

  • Wu projected Q1 2026 total revenues between $52 million to $58 million, with $55 million at the midpoint. She noted, "we anticipate a decline in first quarter revenue from Wuxi to $21 million due to a lower demand from reduced EV subsidies and the Chinese New Year shutdown. However, we expect our revenue from our core business to grow by an impressive 20% sequentially to $34 million at the midpoint."
  • Non-GAAP operating expenses for Q1 are expected at $37 million, with a $0.07 net loss per share based on 223 million shares at the midpoint of the revenue range.
  • Wu stated, "indie is financially well positioned to support our path to strong and profitable growth as design wins ramp through 2026."

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q4 2025 was $58 million, with non-GAAP operating expenses at $36.8 million. The non-GAAP operating loss was $10.1 million, and net loss per share was $0.07. Cash and cash equivalents, including restricted cash, were $155.7 million at quarter end, reflecting a $15.5 million decrease from the previous quarter.
  • Wuxi revenue for Q4 was around $29.7 million. Wu explained the cash decrease included $6.8 million for semi-annual interest payments on convertible notes.

Q&A

  • Cody Grant Acree, The Benchmark Company: Asked for Wuxi revenue and reasons for sequential decline. Wu responded Wuxi revenue was "around $29.7 million," citing "the upcoming Chinese New Year shutdown and the reduced EV subsidies."
  • Acree asked about radar program ramp. McClymont replied, "the OEM traction has just been off the charts, and it gives us a good problem to solve. We need to focus now on making sure that our supply chain is robust enough to support the ramp that we expect."
  • Acree inquired about substrate and packaging constraints. McClymont said, "there was maybe a little bit less than $1 million of demand that is still questionable that we might get or not based on supply, but we've made some significant progress versus Q4 where it affected around $5 million in that quarter."
  • Sujeeva De Silva, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked about backlog and design wins. McClymont stated, "we only really update our strategic backlog once a year...we did make some progress on the sales side and add some new discrete designs out with the larger programs."
  • De Silva asked about non-Wuxi China business. McClymont said, "we're doing well in all regions...we have exposure to OEMs based over all parts of Europe, also in Asia, China, even India."
  • Will (for Jon Tanwanteng), CJS Securities: Asked about opportunity size in robotics and quantum. McClymont responded, "the robotics space is hard to call...we do feel that it can be a very material market as we progress through the rest of this decade. In terms of quantum...we shipped about $1 million worth of optical products in that application in 2025, and we expect maybe around a trebling of that through 2026."
  • Anthony Stoss, Craig-Hallum: Asked for radar revenue expectations for 2026 and OpEx outlook. McClymont replied, "in terms of the radar volume, it's still in that same ZIP code. Nothing really has changed in the short term," and OpEx is expected to be "about flat. Maybe a couple of lumps here and there as we invest in tooling, but no more than $1 million plus/minus."
  • Craig Ellis, B. Riley Securities: Asked about growth drivers for Q1. McClymont said, "radar is still relatively small volume in the last quarter and this quarter," but highlighted ADAS and vision chips as main drivers.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on clarifications regarding Wuxi, supply chain constraints, and the ramp trajectory for radar and vision programs, with a neutral to slightly positive tone and congratulatory remarks on progress.
  • Management maintained a confident outlook on program momentum and supply chain mitigation, frequently expressing optimism, such as "we feel absolutely phenomenal about where we are with the program" (McClymont), but acknowledged ongoing supply constraints and the need for "laser focus."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained neutral to slightly positive with probing questions on supply chain, ramp visibility, and regional performance, while management's confidence appeared steady, emphasizing operational progress and expanding opportunities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue grew sequentially from $53.7 million in Q3 2025 to $58 million in Q4 2025; guidance for Q1 2026 projects core business revenue to rise 20% sequentially.
  • Guidance language in Q4 2025 was more detailed regarding core business growth vs. Wuxi and included explicit expectations for a bounce in Wuxi revenue in Q2 2026.
  • Strategic focus evolved to emphasize scaling radar and vision programs, expanding into humanoid robotics and quantum sensing, and managing supply constraints by broadening the supplier base.
  • Analyst questions continued to focus on supply chain, ramp timelines, and regional business exposure, with increased attention on the impact of reduced EV subsidies and Chinese New Year on Wuxi results.
  • Management's sentiment remained optimistic with a focus on execution and operational discipline, while analysts maintained a pragmatic tone, mirroring the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing supply constraints, particularly for substrates and packaging, noting "we expect the broader supply environment to remain constrained, and we will need to remain laser focused to manage the situation through 2026."
  • Analysts questioned the impact of supply chain constraints on short-term and ramped revenue, as well as risks related to Wuxi revenue from EV subsidy reductions and holiday shutdowns.
  • Management outlined mitigation strategies, including qualifying second source vendors and expanding production and supplier capacity.

Final Takeaway

Indie Semiconductor's management highlights a strong Q4 performance driven by core radar and vision programs, expanding into high-growth markets such as humanoid robotics and quantum sensing. The company anticipates core business revenue to grow 20% sequentially in Q1 2026, while strategically navigating persistent supply chain constraints and progressing toward the closing of the Wuxi divestiture. Management signals continued operational focus and confidence in capturing growth opportunities as ADAS adoption accelerates across global automotive and adjacent sectors.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Danone ADR Non-GAAP EPS of €3.80, revenue of €27.28B; introduces FY26 outlook
data.description:
• Danone ADR press release (DANOY): FY Non-GAAP EPS of €3.80.
  • Revenue of €27.28B (-0.4% Y/Y).
  • €2.8bn Free cash flow, reflecting strong operating performance.
  • Proposed dividend up +4.7% to €2.25.
  • 2026 guidance in line with mid-term ambition: like-for-like sales growth expected between +3% and +5%, with recurring operating income growing faster than sales

data.title: Tandem Diabetes Care targets 10-11% pump shipment growth in 2026 with PayGo transition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tandem Diabetes Care, Inc. (TNDM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO John Sheridan stated, "2025 was a defining year for Tandem, where we surpassed the milestone of $1 billion in sales while delivering on our mission to provide new innovations, improved outcomes and a revolutionary experience to nearly 0.5 million customers worldwide." He highlighted record-setting Q4 results, double-digit growth, and improved profitability. Sheridan emphasized three main initiatives: modernizing commercial operations, delivering new technology, and reshaping the business model. Direct commercial operations launched in the U.K., Switzerland, and Austria, with more European expansion planned for 2026 and 2027.
  • Sheridan outlined technology advances, including the launch of Control-IQ+, new features for t:slim and Mobi, and upcoming launches of Mobi internationally and new sensor integrations. He explained, "We plan to build on this momentum in 2026 and have multiple new products either imminently launching, awaiting regulatory clearance or reaching key milestones in the development path."
  • Sheridan described a strategic shift: "A key aspect of this acceleration is our decision to adopt a pay-as-you-go reimbursement structure, which creates a near-term offset to sales while significantly strengthening our business model over time."
  • CFO Leigh Vosseller said, "2025 was a record year for Tandem, which is highlighted by our milestone achievement of more than $1 billion in worldwide sales and multiple records in Q4, including our highest sales, gross margin and pump shipments." She further noted, "We delivered on our commitment to improving profitability in a meaningful way. We expanded gross margin by 3 percentage points to 54% for the full year and reported our highest quarterly margin ever at 58%."

Outlook

  • Vosseller announced, "Our 2026 U.S. sales are expected to be in the range of $730 million to $745 million based on growth in pump shipments of 10% to 11% year-over-year. This incorporates $70 million to $80 million of pricing headwinds, reflecting our adoption of a pay-as-you-go model."
  • "Pump shipments in the U.S. are expected to increase 10% to 11% year-over-year, returning to new pump growth led by MDI conversions. Renewal pumps are expected to comprise more than half of total shipments."
  • "International sales are expected to be in the range of $335 million to $340 million for the year."
  • "Worldwide sales for 2026 are expected to be in the range of $1.065 billion to $1.085 billion. This incorporates $85 million to $95 million in total sales headwinds associated with our strategic business model changes."
  • Vosseller projected gross margin to "step up to a range of 56% to 57%, scaling from nearly 54% in the first quarter to 60% in the fourth quarter. Adjusted EBITDA is also expected to demonstrate leverage in the range of 5% to 6% for the full year of 2026."

Financial Results

  • Vosseller reported, "In 2025, worldwide sales grew 12%, our second year in a row of double-digit sales growth based on a 10% increase in the U.S. to $707 million and 15% internationally to $308 million."
  • "Our record worldwide sales of $290 million represented 15% year-over-year growth. This is the strongest sales quarter in our company's history."
  • U.S. Q4 sales increased 14% to $210 million, driven by more than 27,000 pump shipments. Pharmacy channel sales nearly doubled from Q3, growing to $16 million or 7% of total U.S. sales.
  • Internationally, Q4 sales reached $80 million, up 17% year-over-year with 11,000 pump shipments. "We also realized benefit from favorable FX, offset by $4 million associated with our transition to direct operations, primarily impacting pump sales."
  • Vosseller highlighted, "Adjusted EBITDA was 11% of sales in the fourth quarter, a 10 percentage point improvement over the prior year. Additionally, we generated our first positive operating margin since 2021 at 3% of sales in Q4."

Q&A

  • Matthew Miksic, Barclays: Asked about the underlying growth excluding PayGo changes. Sheridan: "I think if you look at the overall revenue growth or shipment growth for the year, it's going to be in the line of 10% to 11%. So it's going to be double-digit growth in shipments."
  • Mathew Blackman, TD Cowen: Inquired about pharmacy coverage and contracting progress. Vosseller: "We do have contracts with all of the major PBMs, the top 3, which gets you about 80% of covered lives under contract. But what we're really focused on is the formulary access where we have roughly 1/3 of lives covered today."
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Asked why now for PayGo and long-term pharmacy goals. Sheridan: "We've talked about pharmacy for a while now. And I think it's absolutely the right time to make this transition."
  • Chris Pasquale, Nephron Research: Asked about the international transition timeline. Sheridan: "We're basically using that as a playbook now, and we're going to do the exact same thing this year for the next countries that come in 2027."
  • Danielle Antalffy, UBS: Asked about margin levers. Vosseller: "One of them really comes from Mobi as we continue to build and scale that part of the business. In the long term, Mobi...the manufacturing cost of a Mobi versus the t:slim is 10% to 15% lower."
  • Matthew O'Brien, Piper Sandler: Asked about confidence in new pump shipment growth. Sheridan: "We have high confidence we're going to get this thing approved this year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on PayGo implementation, international expansion, and gross margin progression, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, often congratulating management but seeking specifics on risks and timing.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently using phrases like "we are excited about it," "we feel highly confident," and "we look forward to demonstrating those in the coming quarters." In Q&A, management was occasionally more detailed and explanatory, particularly on operational logistics and transition challenges.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analyst sentiment were more focused on execution and transition risks, though overall confidence in long-term prospects remained strong.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured explicit guidance for 2026, including detailed figures for sales, margins, and pump shipment growth, while the previous quarter stopped short of explicit guidance and focused on setting expectations.
  • The PayGo transition was announced as a major strategic shift, compared to prior incremental pharmacy expansion. Management now targets a majority of shipments through pharmacy in 2-3 years, versus earlier measured steps.
  • Analysts in the current call focused heavily on transition execution, P&L impacts, and international direct operations, compared to prior quarter's questions about pricing uplift and renewal trends.
  • Management's confidence in margin expansion and pipeline strength was consistent, but more forward-looking detail on business model evolution was provided this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited pricing headwinds from the PayGo transition, which are expected to moderate revenue growth in the near term.
  • The transition to direct operations internationally presents short-term sales headwinds from distributor destocking and inventory buybacks.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about the timing and execution of PayGo, attrition rates, and the pace of international direct model adoption.
  • Management described mitigation strategies, including leveraging the large installed base to offset revenue impact, expanding formulary access, and scaling pharmacy and direct operations gradually.

Final Takeaway

Tandem Diabetes Care closed 2025 with record-breaking results and outlined a transformative shift to a pay-as-you-go business model, targeting 10-11% pump shipment growth in 2026 and further expansion of pharmacy and international direct operations. Management emphasized that while near-term revenue and margin headwinds are expected during this transition, long-term value creation, enhanced profitability, and accelerated growth are anticipated as the new model scales and new innovations reach the market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: L'Air Liquide S.A. GAAP EPS of €6.10, revenue of €26.94B; sets FY26 targets
data.description:
• L'Air Liquide S.A. press release (AIQUF): FY GAAP EPS of €6.10.
  • Revenue of €26.94B (-0.4% Y/Y).
  • Recurring ROCE increased to 11.2%.
  • Backlog now stands at €4.9 billion.
  • At the Annual General Meeting on May 5, 2026, the payment of a dividend of 3.70 euros per share will be proposed to shareholders for the fiscal year 2025. The proposed dividend is up +12.1% compared to the previous year and up +38% over the last 3 years.
  • Outlook: In 2026, Air Liquide is very confident in its ability to increase its operating margin by +100 basis points and to deliver recurring net profit growth, at constant exchange rates. To provide enhanced visibility on its long-term trajectory, Air Liquide is raising and extending its margin expansion ambition, demonstrating the Group's confidence in its operational excellence:Confirmation of the 2026 objective for operating margin improvement of +100 basis points, excluding the energy impact;
  • Introduction of a new objective for 2027 to improve the operating margin by +100 basis points, excluding the energy impact;
  • The cumulative ambition thus stands at +560 basis points over the six-year period, 2022-2027.
  • To outline the levers of this long-term financial trajectory, the Group will host a Capital Markets Day in H2 2026

data.title: UK retail sales rise 4.5% in January
data.description:
• Retail sales in the United Kingdom increased 4.50 percent in January of 2026 over the same month in the previous year, beating concensus of 2.8% growth.
  • Retail sales in the United Kingdom increased 1.80 percent in January of 2026 over the previous month. 

  • ETFs: (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB)
  • Currency: (GBP:USD)
